question: Can you kimchi?
title: Can You Make Kimchi at Home? Everything You Need to Know | Scotty's Gardening Lab
metaDescription: Yes, you can kimchi — and it's easier than you think. Learn how kimchi fermentation works, what ingredients you need, and why this ancient food is so good for you.
content:
People look at a jar of kimchi and they think: this is complicated. This is something you need a Korean grandmother to teach you, a special kitchen setup, some mysterious technique passed down through generations. And while I have nothing but respect for Korean grandmothers — they know things about fermentation that I am still learning — the truth is that kimchi is one of the most approachable fermented foods a person can make at home.

You absolutely can kimchi. And once you understand what's actually happening during fermentation, you'll wonder why you ever hesitated.

---

**The short answer:** Kimchi is a fermented vegetable food, traditionally made from Napa cabbage, daikon radish, garlic, ginger, and hot pepper paste, preserved and transformed by naturally occurring lactic acid bacteria. It ferments at room temperature in one to two days, or more slowly in the refrigerator over weeks. The process is straightforward, the science is fascinating, and the health benefits are backed by substantial research.

---

## What Kimchi Actually Is

Let me give you the real story on kimchi, because it's a little bit more interesting than just "spicy Korean cabbage."

Kimchi is a traditional fermented vegetable food that originated in Korea as a preservation method long before refrigeration existed. The base — most commonly Napa cabbage — gets brined with salt to draw out moisture and create an environment where beneficial bacteria can thrive. Then it gets mixed with seasonings: garlic, ginger, green onions, and the signature gochugaru hot pepper paste. Traditional versions also include fish sauce or salted shrimp for an umami depth.

Research published in PMC by the National Institutes of Health describes kimchi as a complex ecosystem. During fermentation, lactic acid bacteria — primarily Leuconostoc, Lactobacillus, and Weissella species — dominate the process. They eat the sugars in the cabbage and other vegetables, producing lactic acid as a byproduct. That lactic acid is what gives kimchi its characteristic sour tang and what preserves the vegetables by lowering the pH to a point where harmful bacteria can't survive.

The result isn't just preserved cabbage. It's a completely transformed food — a new flavor profile, a new texture, and a nutritional fingerprint that the raw ingredients don't have on their own.

## The Three Core Styles

Now, kimchi isn't one thing. There are hundreds of regional variations across Korea, and when I make kimchi at my operation, I focus on three styles that cover a wide range of preferences.

Traditional kimchi uses Napa cabbage, daikon radish, carrots, onions, garlic, ginger, green onions, and fish sauce in a gochugaru (hot pepper) paste. This is what most people think of when they hear the word kimchi — it's vibrant, complex, sour, spicy, and deeply savory. The fish sauce provides glutamates and amino acids that feed the fermentation and add that layered umami that makes you keep going back for more.

Vegan kimchi uses the same vegetable base and the same pepper paste but omits the fish sauce. The fermentation is still driven by the naturally occurring bacteria on the vegetables themselves. You lose a little of that glutamate depth, but the lactic acid fermentation still produces a complex, sour, beautifully balanced product. OSU Extension's kimchi basics resource notes that the salt concentration — ranging from 2 to 5 percent — is the key variable that controls which organisms dominate, regardless of whether you're using animal-derived ingredients.

White kimchi is kimchi without the hot pepper. No spice whatsoever. It's all the flavor — the sour, the fermented depth, the garlic and ginger — without the heat. This one surprises people. They expect something bland, and they get something complex and refreshing. It's particularly good for people who love the gut health benefits of kimchi but can't tolerate spicy foods.

## The Fermentation Science

Here's where it gets genuinely exciting, at least for someone who thinks about this stuff as much as I do.

When you pack your kimchi into a jar and leave it at room temperature, you're creating conditions that favor anaerobic lactic acid bacteria while suppressing everything else. The salt draws water out of the vegetables through osmosis, creating a brine. That brine, combined with the anaerobic environment of a packed jar, is exactly what Lactobacillus species love.

A 2014 study published in the Journal of Medicinal Food and indexed in PubMed documented the extensive health functionality of kimchi, describing anticancer properties, antiobesity effects, cholesterol reduction, antioxidative activity, and probiotic properties. A separate PMC study on kimchi's effects on serum lipid profiles found statistically significant improvements in healthy young adults who consumed kimchi regularly.

The fermentation also increases bioavailability. Fermentation breaks down the plant cell walls that would otherwise limit how much of the vegetables' nutrients your body can actually absorb. The bacteria produce enzymes that do a kind of pre-digestion, making the antioxidants, organic acids, and functional metabolites more accessible. You eat the same vegetables, but you get more from them.

The University of Georgia's CAES Field Report on homemade kimchi notes that fermentation increases the ability to access antioxidants, organic acids, and functional metabolites such as gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA) and exopolysaccharides — compounds associated with reduced inflammation and improved gut and cognitive health.

## How the Process Works Step by Step

I want to walk you through the conceptual process so you understand what's happening, not just what to do.

First, you salt the cabbage. This step takes anywhere from one to several hours and it's doing important work. The salt draws moisture out of the cabbage through osmosis, creating the brine that will eventually submerge the whole mixture. It's also softening the cell walls, allowing the paste to penetrate. And it's beginning the process of selection — the salt concentration you choose determines which bacteria can survive.

Second, you mix in the paste. Garlic, ginger, green onions, gochugaru — these aren't just flavor. Garlic contains allicin, which has antimicrobial properties that help suppress harmful bacteria while not significantly impacting the salt-tolerant lactobacillus species that drive fermentation. The gochugaru itself is a fermentation partner — its capsaicin compounds influence the microbial community in ways that researchers are still unpacking.

Third, you pack it. Tight packing is important. You're eliminating air pockets to create the anaerobic environment the lactic acid bacteria need. This is where the phrase "packing kimchi" comes from — the physical act is doing real biological work.

Fourth, you wait. At room temperature (around 65–75 degrees Fahrenheit), active fermentation begins within hours and produces a noticeably sour kimchi within one to two days. Move it to the refrigerator and the process slows dramatically, allowing for a gentler, more complex fermentation over weeks to months. The longer it goes in the fridge, the more sour and developed the flavor becomes.

## What Kimchi Tastes Like at Different Stages

This is something I get asked a lot, and I love talking about it because the flavor journey of kimchi is genuinely remarkable.

Fresh kimchi — right after mixing, before significant fermentation — is bright and crunchy. You taste the cabbage, the garlic, the pepper. The sourness is minimal. This stage is actually quite good and some people prefer to eat it right here.

After one to two days at room temperature, the lactic acid bacteria have gotten to work. The sourness develops — a clean, bright, lactic sourness that's very different from vinegar. The flavors integrate and deepen. This is what you'd get from a batch of kimchi you just made.

After weeks in the refrigerator, the flavors develop further. The sourness deepens and mellows simultaneously. The garlic and ginger integrate into the overall flavor rather than standing out sharply. The texture softens a bit. This is kimchi in its mature form — complex, layered, deeply fermented.

Well-aged kimchi has an almost funky, fermented depth that works beautifully cooked into kimchi jjigae (stew) or kimchi fried rice. At this point, the fermentation has done its most transformative work.

## Why You Should Be Making This

Beyond the flavor — and the flavor really is worth it — the health case for fermented foods is getting stronger every year.

A landmark 2021 study from Stanford Medicine found that a fermented food diet increases gut microbiome diversity and decreases molecular markers of inflammation. The researchers specifically called out kimchi and fermented vegetables among the foods that produced these benefits. Microbial diversity is one of the strongest indicators of overall gut health we have.

Kimchi provides both prebiotic fiber (food for your gut bacteria) and probiotic microbes (live bacteria) in the same food. That combination — what researchers sometimes call a synbiotic effect — is particularly powerful for supporting the gut microbiome.

And here's something I find fascinating from the research side. The bacteria in kimchi have been fighting their own microbial wars for dominance in that jar. They've outcompeted every other organism in that environment. When you eat them, they bring that competitive edge into your gut. They can potentially educate your existing gut bacteria — pass along information about how to outcompete pathogenic organisms and how to more efficiently break down your food.

Make the kimchi. Your gut will thank you.

---

scottyQuotes: We do a traditional with fish sauce, a vegan with all the same ingredients minus the fish sauce, and a white kimchi — all the vegetables, all the flavor, none of the spice. Something for everybody.
Kimchi is one of the most approachable fermented foods a person can make at home. Once you understand what's happening during fermentation, you'll wonder why you ever hesitated.
The bacteria in that jar have been winning their own microbial wars. When you eat them, they bring that competitive edge into your gut.
